title: Inpatient Hospice: Comfort Care in the Last Days of Life
subtitle: A 62-year-old with end-stage pancreatic cancer is admitted to inpatient hospice with refractory pain, nausea, terminal delirium, and noisy secretions. The nurse provides comfort-focused symptom relief and prepares the family for the last hours.

patient.name: S. Whitfield
patient.admitDate: Inpatient hospice. End-stage metastatic pancreatic cancer transferred from home hospice for refractory pain, nausea, new terminal delirium, and caregiver fatigue. Goals are comfort and dignity; family present and aligned.

labs.v: Match the antiemetic to the cause.

labs.r: Teach family it is not distress.

nursesNote: S. Whitfield, 62, has end-stage metastatic pancreatic cancer and is admitted to inpatient hospice for refractory pain, nausea, new terminal delirium, and caregiver fatigue; his POLST directs DNR/DNI and an allow-natural-death, comfort-focused plan, and his family is present and aligned. The nurse manages symptoms by mechanism: scheduled plus breakthrough opioids titrated to comfort (no arbitrary ceiling) with a bowel regimen started alongside them, an antiemetic matched to the cause of the nausea, low-dose haloperidol with calm presence for terminal delirium, repositioning and glycopyrrolate for noisy terminal secretions, and a low-dose opioid with a fan for air hunger. Nonpharmacologic comfort — mouth care, gentle positioning, a quiet room, and family presence — is provided throughout. The family is taught what to expect in the last hours (mottling, Cheyne-Stokes breathing, declining responsiveness) and reassured that the 'death rattle' does not mean the patient is suffering. Caregiver fatigue is addressed with respite, and spiritual care and 13-month bereavement support are arranged.
providerOrders: Maintain comfort-focused care per POLST (DNR/DNI, allow natural death)
Titrate scheduled and breakthrough opioids to comfort; start a bowel regimen
Treat nausea, terminal delirium, secretions, dyspnea, and anxiety by mechanism
Provide nonpharmacologic comfort (mouth care, positioning, calm environment)
Educate and support the family; arrange spiritual care, respite, and bereavement follow-up

opts.text: Hospice focuses on comfort, not cure, for a prognosis of about six months or less
Opioid pain at end of life is titrated to comfort without an arbitrary dose ceiling
Every opioid should be paired with a bowel regimen
Terminal secretions are managed with repositioning and an antisecretory drug
Comfort medications should be withheld to keep the patient alert
Antiemetics are chosen based on the cause of the nausea
Low-dose opioids relieve dyspnea and air hunger at end of life
Terminal delirium is managed by treating reversible causes and low-dose haloperidol with presence
Families should be taught what to expect in the last hours (mottling, Cheyne-Stokes)
Caregiver fatigue is addressed with respite and bereavement support
Terminal secretions mean the patient is choking and suffering
Lorazepam can ease end-of-life anxiety

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the response to distress on admission

opts.text: Assess comfort (pain, nausea, dyspnea, secretions, agitation)
Titrate opioids and targeted symptom medications to comfort
Add nonpharmacologic comfort (positioning, calm room, mouth care)
Educate and support the family on what to expect
Coordinate ongoing comfort, spiritual care, and bereavement
Give scheduled and breakthrough opioids titrated to comfort
Start a bowel regimen with the opioids
Reposition and give glycopyrrolate for terminal secretions
Provide a low-dose opioid and a fan for air hunger
Withhold pain medicine to avoid 'hastening death'
Insert a feeding tube and start IV fluids to prolong life
Restrain the patient for terminal restlessness
Suction deeply and repeatedly for the 'death rattle'

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Relieve pain and distressing symptoms with titrated medications
Provide mouth care, positioning, and a calm environment
Treat terminal delirium and secretions appropriately
Prepare the family for the last hours and support them
Arrange spiritual care, respite, and bereavement follow-up
The patient is calm and comfortable after opioid and antiemetic titration
The family is taught that mottling and Cheyne-Stokes breathing are expected
Glycopyrrolate and repositioning reduce terminal secretions
Bereavement support is offered to the family
Opioids are withheld for fear of hastening death despite the patient's pain
CPR is started when the DNR patient stops breathing
The patient is deeply suctioned repeatedly for the death rattle
A feeding tube is placed to 'keep up strength'
